claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 3 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me start by asking about your younger child, Blake, who is 3 years old.

Does Blake currently live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives with me here in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. And does Alex, your 17-year-old, also live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me as well, here at home in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex's situation specifically, since they're 17 years old.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They left school and aren't doing A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask a few more questions about Alex's situation.

Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week do they work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One more question about Alex - is Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or with the armed services? (This would be relevant if they left education or training within the last 20 weeks)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with a careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

**For Blake (3 years old): You ARE eligible ✓**
Blake is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for them. This meets the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

**For Alex (17 years old): You are NOT eligible ✗**
Unfortunately, you don't qualify for Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is over 16 years old
- Alex is not currently in approved education or training
- Alex has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services

For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue if they stay in approved education or training. There's also a 20-week extension available if a 16 or 17-year-old leaves education and registers with a careers service or the armed services, but Alex hasn't done this.

**What this means:** You can claim Child Benefit for Blake, but not for Alex. If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if they start approved education, training, or register with a careers service), you could potentially become eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if the child is under 16, OR if they are under 20 and stay in approved education or training. Alex could also qualify for the 20-week extension if they had registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services after leaving education, but they have not done so. Since Alex is over 16, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. Since Blake is under 16 years old and lives with you, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, you qualify for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (3). Both children live with the claimant in the UK (Manchester), and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Blake meets the standard eligibility criteria as they are under 16 and live with the claimant. However, Alex does not meet the criteria for 16-19 year olds, as they are not in approved education or training and have not registered with a careers service or armed services following leaving education. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only.

Since Alex is over 16, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. Since Blake is under 16 years old and lives with you, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, you qualify for Child Benefit for Blake.
